GEN 1:1.text: KJV 'In the beginning' translates Hebrew 'bereshit', the opening word of the Bible. The KJV follows the Hebrew word order closely.
'God' here is Hebrew 'Elohim', a plural noun used with singular verbs, suggesting the Trinity. KJV capitalizes 'God' as a proper noun.
'created' is Hebrew 'bara', used only for divine creation. KJV preserves the distinction between 'create' (bara) and 'make' (asah).

GEN 1:2.text: 'without form, and void' translates Hebrew 'tohu wabohu' - chaos and emptiness. KJV uses alliteration to capture the Hebrew poetry.
'the deep' is Hebrew 'tehom', related to the Babylonian 'Tiamat'. KJV uses 'deep' rather than 'abyss' to avoid mythological connotations.
'moved' translates Hebrew 'rachaph', to hover or brood like a bird over eggs. KJV captures this maternal imagery.

GEN 1:26.text: 'Let us make' - The plural pronoun has been interpreted as the Trinity, angels, or plural of majesty. KJV preserves the plural without interpretation.
'image' is Hebrew 'tselem', meaning representation or likeness. KJV distinguishes 'image' from 'likeness' in verse 27.
'man' is Hebrew 'adam', related to 'adamah' (ground). KJV uses 'man' as generic humanity, not just the individual Adam.

GEN 2:7.text: 'formed' is Hebrew 'yatsar', used for a potter shaping clay. KJV emphasizes God's hands-on creation.
'breath of life' is Hebrew 'nishmat chayyim'. KJV distinguishes this from 'spirit' (ruach) used elsewhere.
'living soul' - Hebrew 'nephesh chayyah'. KJV uses 'soul' here, though 'nephesh' also means creature or being.

GEN 3:15.text: 'bruise' - Hebrew 'shuf', to crush or strike at. KJV uses 'bruise' for both the serpent and the seed, though the Hebrew suggests different intensities.
'seed' is Hebrew 'zera', singular collective. KJV preserves the singular, pointing to Christ as the ultimate seed.
This verse is called the 'Protoevangelium' - the first gospel promise. KJV translators recognized its messianic significance.

GEN 12:1.text: 'Get thee out' - Hebrew 'lech lecha', literally 'go for yourself' or 'go to yourself'. KJV captures the command and purpose.
Abram was 75 years old when he left Haran (v. 4). KJV narrative shows God's call comes regardless of age.

GEN 15:6.text: 'believed' - Hebrew 'he'emin', from root 'aman' (firm, faithful). KJV 'believed' captures the sense of trust and reliance.
'counted it to him for righteousness' - Hebrew 'chashab', to reckon or impute. KJV uses 'counted' for legal/accounting terminology.
Quoted by Paul in Romans 4:3, Galatians 3:6, and James 2:23 as foundational for justification by faith.

EXO 3:14.text: 'I AM THAT I AM' - Hebrew 'Ehyeh asher ehyeh'. KJV preserves the first person, connecting to the divine name YHWH (He is).
'I AM' becomes a title for Christ in John's Gospel (John 8:58). KJV uses small caps LORD for YHWH elsewhere.
The name indicates God's self-existence and eternal nature. KJV captures this with the timeless 'I AM'.

EXO 20:3.text: First Commandment: 'Thou shalt have no other gods before me.' KJV 'before me' means 'in my presence' or 'besides me'.
'before me' is Hebrew 'al panay', literally 'upon my face'. KJV captures the sense of exclusive worship.

EXO 20:7.text: Third Commandment: KJV 'take the name of the LORD thy God in vain' - using God's name for false oaths or empty purposes.
'vain' is Hebrew 'shav', meaning emptiness, falsehood, or worthlessness. KJV captures the prohibition against empty use.

PSA 23:1.text: 'LORD' in KJV small caps represents the divine name YHWH (Yahweh/Jehovah), distinguishing it from 'Lord' (Adonai).
'I shall not want' - Hebrew 'lo echsar', I will not lack. KJV 'want' means 'lack' in Elizabethan English.
David wrote this psalm based on his experience as a shepherd boy (1 Samuel 16:11).

PSA 23:4.text: 'valley of the shadow of death' - Hebrew 'gey tsalmavet', valley of deep darkness. KJV 'shadow of death' captures both meanings.
'thy rod and thy staff' - The rod for protection, the staff for guidance. KJV preserves the shepherd's tools.
'comfort' is Hebrew 'nacham', to console or have compassion. KJV emphasizes divine comfort in danger.

PSA 51:10.text: 'Create' is Hebrew 'bara', used only for divine creation. KJV preserves this strong term for spiritual renewal.
'right spirit' - Hebrew 'ruach nachon', a steadfast or established spirit. KJV 'right' means 'correct' or 'proper'.
David wrote this psalm after his sin with Bathsheba (2 Samuel 12). KJV preserves the personal confession.

PSA 119:11.text: 'hid' is Hebrew 'tsaphan', to treasure or store up. KJV captures the sense of valuing and protecting God's Word.
'heart' in Hebrew includes mind, will, and emotions. KJV 'heart' encompasses the whole inner being.
This acrostic psalm has 22 sections for each letter of the Hebrew alphabet. KJV preserves the structure.

ISA 7:14.text: 'virgin' translates Hebrew 'almah', a young woman of marriageable age. KJV uses 'virgin' based on context and LXX translation.
'Immanuel' means 'God with us'. KJV preserves the Hebrew name as a title of the Messiah.
Quoted in Matthew 1:23 as fulfilled in Jesus' birth. KJV Matthew explicitly connects this to Christ.

ISA 9:6.text: 'Wonderful, Counsellor' - Could be read as two titles or one: 'Wonderful Counsellor'. KJV follows Hebrew punctuation.
'mighty God' is Hebrew 'El Gibbor', Mighty God. KJV capitalizes 'God', identifying the child as divine.
'Everlasting Father' - Hebrew 'Avi Ad', Father of Eternity or Eternal Father. KJV emphasizes the messianic title.

ISA 53:5.text: 'wounded' is Hebrew 'chalal', pierced or profaned. KJV captures the violence done to the Servant.
'stripes' is Hebrew 'chabburah', bruises or welts. KJV emphasizes the physical suffering.
This chapter is the great Messianic prophecy of the suffering Servant. KJV preserves its application to Christ.

MAT 1:23.text: KJV follows Isaiah 7:14 closely: 'they shall call his name Emmanuel'. The fulfillment formula is characteristic of Matthew.
'Emmanuel' is Greek 'Emmanuel', from Hebrew 'Immanu El'. KJV preserves the Hebrew form of the name.

MAT 5:3.text: 'Blessed' is Greek 'makarios', happy or fortunate. KJV 'blessed' captures the divine favor.
'poor in spirit' is Greek 'ptochos to pneumati', spiritually destitute. KJV emphasizes spiritual poverty.

MAT 5:44.text: 'Love your enemies' - Greek 'agapao', self-sacrificing love. KJV distinguishes this from 'phileo' (friendship love).
KJV 'pray for them which despitefully use you' captures the Greek 'epeRXomai', to persecute or harass.

MAT 6:9.text: 'Our Father' - Greek 'Pater hemon'. KJV preserves the familial intimacy of Jesus' teaching on prayer.
'Hallowed be thy name' - Greek 'hagiastheto to onoma sou'. KJV uses 'hallowed' (made holy) rather than 'sanctified'.

MAT 6:33.text: 'Seek ye first' - Greek 'zeteite proton', seek first. KJV emphasizes priority: God's kingdom before material needs.
'righteousness' is Greek 'dikaiosyne', right standing with God. KJV distinguishes this from works-righteousness.

MAT 11:28.text: 'Come unto me' - Greek 'deute pros me', come here to me. KJV captures the invitation to approach Jesus.
'heavy laden' is Greek 'pephortismenoi', loaded down. KJV emphasizes the burden of religious legalism.
'rest' is Greek 'anapausis', cessation from labor and refreshment. KJV captures both meanings.

MAT 28:19.text: 'in the name' is singular in Greek, indicating one God. KJV preserves the Trinitarian formula.
'Father, Son, Holy Ghost' - The Great Commission in Trinitarian form. KJV preserves this foundational formula.

JHN 1:1.text: 'In the beginning' - Greek 'en arche', echoing Genesis 1:1. KJV emphasizes Christ's eternal existence.
'Word' is Greek 'Logos', divine reason or expression. KJV preserves this philosophical and theological term.
'the Word was God' - Greek 'theos en ho logos'. KJV preserves the word order: 'God' is the predicate, emphasizing deity.

JHN 1:14.text: 'made flesh' - Greek 'sarx egeneto', became flesh. KJV emphasizes the incarnation.
'dwelt' is Greek 'eskenosen', tabernacled or pitched tent. KJV captures the imagery of God's presence.
'full of grace and truth' - Greek 'charis kai aletheia', covenant love and faithfulness. KVL preserves the Hebrew concept.

JHN 3:3.text: 'born again' - Greek 'gennethe anothen', born from above. KJV captures both meanings: again and from above.
'cannot see' is Greek 'ou dunatai idein', is not able to see. KJV emphasizes inability without new birth.

JHN 3:16.text: 'so loved' - Greek 'houtos egapesen', loved in this manner. KJV emphasizes the extent of God's love.
'only begotten' is Greek 'monogenes', one and only, unique. KJV emphasizes Christ's unique sonship.
'whosoever' is Greek 'pas ho pisteuon', everyone believing. KJV emphasizes the universal offer.

JHN 14:6.text: 'I am the way' - Greek 'ego eimi he hodos'. KJV preserves the definite article: Jesus is THE way, not a way.
'no man cometh unto the Father, but by me' - Exclusive claim. KJV preserves the necessity of Christ for salvation.

ROM 3:23.text: 'all have sinned' - Greek 'pantes hemarton', all sinned. KJV emphasizes universal sinfulness.
'come short' is Greek 'hysterountai', fall short or lack. KJV uses an accounting term: falling short of the standard.

ROM 5:8.text: 'commendeth' is Greek 'sunistesin', demonstrates or proves. KJV emphasizes God demonstrating His love.
'while we were yet sinners' - Greek 'eti hamartolon onton hemon'. KJV emphasizes the timing: Christ died for us while we were still sinners.

ROM 6:23.text: 'wages' is Greek 'opsonia', soldier's pay. KJV uses a military metaphor: sin pays death as wages.
'gift' is Greek 'charisma', free gift of grace. KJV contrasts earned wages with free grace.

ROM 8:28.text: 'work together' is Greek 'synergei', synergy. KJV emphasizes God causing all things to cooperate for good.
'love God' and 'called according to his purpose' - The promise is for believers, not all people universally.

ROM 10:9.text: 'confess with thy mouth' - Greek 'homologeseis to stomati sou'. KJV emphasizes public confession.
'raised from the dead' - The resurrection is essential to saving faith. KJV preserves this requirement.

ROM 10:13.text: 'whosoever' is Greek 'pas gar', for everyone. KJV emphasizes the universal invitation.
'call upon' is Greek 'epikalesetai', invoke or appeal to. KJV captures the sense of calling for help.
Quotes Joel 2:32. KJV preserves the Old Testament connection.

1CO 13:4-8.text: 'Charity' is Greek 'agape', self-sacrificing love. KJV uses 'charity' in its older sense of Christian love.
15 characteristics of love in this passage. KJV preserves the detailed description of true love.

EPH 2:8.text: 'by grace' - Greek 'te gar chariti', by the grace. KJV emphasizes grace as the means of salvation.
'through faith' - Greek 'dia pisteos'. KJV distinguishes the source (grace) from the channel (faith).
'gift' is Greek 'doron', a present. KJV emphasizes that salvation is completely God's gift, not human achievement.

PHP 4:13.text: 'I can do all things' - Greek 'panta ischyo', I am strong for all things. KJV emphasizes empowerment.
'Christ which strengtheneth me' - Greek 'ton endynamounta me'. KJV emphasizes Christ as the source of strength.
Context (vv. 11-12) shows Paul means contentment in all circumstances, not ability to do anything he wants.

HEB 4:12.text: 'quick' is Elizabethan English for 'living' or 'alive'. KJV emphasizes the living nature of God's Word.
'twoedged sword' is Greek 'machaira distomos', double-mouthed sword. KJV emphasizes the penetrating power.
'discerner' is Greek 'kritikos', critic or judge. KJV emphasizes Scripture's ability to judge thoughts.

HEB 11:1.text: 'substance' is Greek 'hypostasis', assurance or confidence. KJV uses 'substance' in its older sense of 'that which stands under'.
'evidence' is Greek 'elenchos', conviction or proof. KJV emphasizes faith as the proof of unseen realities.

JAS 1:22.text: 'be ye doers' - Greek 'poietai', doers or makers. KJV emphasizes action over mere hearing.
'deceiving your own selves' - Greek 'paralogizomenoi heautous', reasoning beside yourselves. KJV emphasizes self-deception.

1PE 3:15.text: 'sanctify the Lord God in your hearts' - Greek 'kyrion de ton christon hagiasate'. KJV emphasizes setting Christ apart as Lord.
'answer' is Greek 'apologian', defense or reasoned response. KJV gives us the word 'apologetics'.
'meekness and fear' - Greek 'prauteti kai phoboi', gentleness and reverence. KJV emphasizes humble respect.

1JN 1:9.text: 'confess' is Greek 'homologomen', agree with or acknowledge. KJV emphasizes agreeing with God about sin.
'faithful and just' - God forgives because He is faithful to His promise and just because of Christ's payment.
'cleanse' is Greek 'katharise', purify. KJV emphasizes complete cleansing from all unrighteousness.

REV 1:8.text: 'Alpha and Omega' - First and last letters of Greek alphabet. KJV uses these as titles of eternality.
'which is, and which was, and which is to come' - Greek 'ho on kai ho en kai ho erchomenos'. KJV preserves the divine name.

REV 3:20.text: 'I stand at the door, and knock' - Greek 'he steko epi ten thyran kai krouo'. KJV presents Christ seeking entrance.
'sup with him' - Greek 'deipneso met' autou', dine with him. KJV captures the intimacy of fellowship with Christ.
